food_name: Pide with Pepperoni (Turkish Flatbread with Cured Salami and Mozzarella)
aliases: Pide with Turkish Sun-Dried Salami and Mozzarella
Pide with cured meat and cheese

english: Pide with Turkish Sun-Dried Salami and Mozzarella
Pide with cured meat and cheese

food_subgroup: Flatbread-based baked dish with cured meat and cheese

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Turkish pide. FOODON_00002572 (flatbread food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Turkish pide. A00LC (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible parent. Pide is a leavened flatbread baked with toppings.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Turkish pide or this specific flatbread-with-topping preparation.

preparation_method: topped with cured meat and cheese

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole pide flatbread with pepperoni and mozzarella, approximately 350 g, as typically served at a Turkish restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pizza with meat topping, regular crust (SR Legacy) and Turkish pide component estimation via USDA Foundation Foods for bread dough, cured meat, and mozzarella
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for Turkish pide with pepperoni. Values are estimated by component-based calculation using USDA FoodData Central entries: (1) Bread, pita, white, unenriched (FDC ID 320305) for the dough base; (2) Salami, dry or hard, pork (FDC ID 174608) for the cured salami topping; (3) Cheese, mozzarella, whole milk (FDC ID 170845) for the cheese layer. Proportions estimated as approximately 55% dough, 25% cured salami, 20% mozzarella by weight, consistent with typical Turkish pide construction. Values are per 100 g of the assembled, baked product.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Pita bread, white (GI 57) used as analogous estimate for Turkish pide dough base.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Turkish pide with pepperoni and mozzarella. White pita bread (GI 57, medium) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 is used as the closest analogous food for the dough component. The addition of protein-rich mozzarella and fat-rich cured salami toppings is expected to moderate the glycemic response slightly below that of plain pita, but GI 57 is retained as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 103.25) / 100 ≈ 58.9; however, given the mixed-dish nature and fat/protein blunting effect, GL per serving (350 g) is estimated at approximately 47 using available carbohydrate of 82.5 g per serving × 57 / 100 ≈ 47.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from cured salami and cheese
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid pide pepperoni due to its high sodium content (approximately 680 mg per 100 g, equating to ~2,380 mg per full serving), which substantially contributes to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index refined wheat dough with moderate carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume pide pepperoni in small portions, as the refined wheat dough carries a medium GI of approximately 57 and a high glycemic load per full serving; the protein and fat from mozzarella and cured salami partially moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from cured salami and mozzarella
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit pide pepperoni due to its combined saturated fat content (approximately 4.9 g per 100 g) and high sodium load from the cured salami and mozzarella, both of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pide Pepperoni is a Turkish flatbread dish consisting of a freshly made, hand-shaped, boat-style leavened wheat dough base topped with Turkish sun-dried salami (a spiced cured meat similar to sucuk or dry salami) and melted mozzarella cheese, then baked in a high-temperature oven until the crust is golden and the cheese is bubbling. Pide is a traditional Turkish baked bread that predates modern pizza and is a staple of Turkish cuisine, commonly served as a main meal. The pepperoni variant served in Singapore at Turkish restaurants such as Sultan Palace adapts the classic pide format with a cured salami topping that appeals to international palates. The dish is energy-dense, providing approximately 272 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution from both the cheese and cured meat, and a moderate-to-high fat content driven by the saturated fat in mozzarella and the cured salami. The sodium content is clinically significant at approximately 680 mg per 100 g, making a full serving a high-sodium meal. The glycemic index is estimated at 57 (medium) based on the white pita bread analogue, with the protein and fat toppings expected to partially blunt the postprandial glucose response. Clinically, this dish warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, and refined carbohydrate content. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten or dairy.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Turkish pide with pepperoni exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for pita bread dough, dry salami, and mozzarella cheese using plausible proportions. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (white pita bread, GI 57, University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (pita bread dough, dry salami, mozzarella) and are not from a direct measurement of this specific dish. A laboratory analysis or HPB-style direct measurement would be required to upgrade confidence to high. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported across all component sources used for this estimation. 5. Vitamin C is 0.0 as this dish contains no significant plant-based vitamin C sources; confirmed effectively zero by component analysis. 6. GI is estimated at 57 using white pita bread as the closest approved analogous food from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008; the actual GI of the assembled dish may be slightly lower due to fat and protein blunting. 7. The is_halal flag is set to true based on the restaurant context (Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine, Singapore), but individual consumers should verify halal certification. 8. The food_name has been normalised from the menu-style '90. Pide Pepperoni' to a canonical descriptive English name. 9. Turkish cuisine does not require mandatory multilingual names under the Veritas v2 multilingual matrix (which mandates additional languages only for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes); therefore local_names fields for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are null. The romanized_local field captures the Turkish original name.
